era_context: Roman Empire under Antoninus Pius (r. 138–161 CE) at the height of the Pax Romana. The reign is marked by relative peace and stability, though frontier pressures are building. Eastern Han Dynasty in China is in the reign of Emperor Andi (r. 132–146 CE), a period of court factionalism and eunuch influence. The Parthian Empire is consolidating power. In India, the Satavahana Empire is in its later phase.

events.title: Construction of the Antonine Wall begins in Britain
events.description: Emperor Antoninus Pius orders the construction of a new frontier wall in Britain, approximately 100 miles north of Hadrian's Wall. The Antonine Wall, built of turf and stone, stretches approximately 37 miles across central Scotland from the Firth of Forth to the Firth of Clyde. The project is directed by the governor Lollius Urbicus and involves the construction of forts, fortlets, and a military road (the Military Way). This represents a significant Roman military investment and a northward expansion of the frontier, reflecting Roman confidence and the desire to expand territorial control in Britain.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from the Antonine Wall (excavations, pottery, coins, inscriptions)
Historia Augusta, Life of Antoninus Pius
Coins and pottery deposits from Antonine Wall forts

events.certainty_note: Archaeological evidence strongly confirms construction activity beginning in 142 CE. Pottery and coin deposits date the occupation to the Antonine period (138–161 CE). The Historia Augusta is late (4th century) but corroborates the general narrative.

events.title: Emperor Andi of Eastern Han continues reign amid court factionalism
events.description: Emperor Andi of the Eastern Han (r. 132–146 CE) continues his reign in 142 CE, a period marked by intense court factionalism and the growing influence of eunuchs and the imperial harem. The emperor is increasingly sidelined by regents and powerful court factions, a pattern that will characterize the late Eastern Han. This period sees the erosion of imperial authority and the rise of regional warlords, though the collapse is still decades away.

sources.name: Book of Han (Hou Hanshu), compiled by Fan Ye (5th century CE)
Records of the Three Kingdoms (Sanguozhi), by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)

events.certainty_note: The broad pattern of court factionalism and eunuch ascendancy during Emperor Andi's reign is well-attested in later compilations. However, specific events and dates for 142 CE are not always precisely documented.

events.title: Vologases III consolidates Parthian power; Silk Road trade flourishes
events.description: The Parthian Empire, under Vologases III (r. 147–191 CE, though some sources place his accession earlier), consolidates control over the crucial Silk Road trade routes connecting China, India, and the Roman Empire. Parthian merchants and intermediaries profit enormously from the transit trade in silk, spices, and luxury goods. This economic dominance is a key source of Parthian power and wealth. Archaeological evidence from caravan cities shows intensive commercial activity.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Silk Road sites

events.certainty_note: The Parthian control of Silk Road trade is well-documented in Roman sources and confirmed by archaeological evidence of trade goods and coins across Central Asia.
cross_references: 166-001 (Roman embassy to China via Parthian routes)

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the Antonine Wall construction is debated; some scholars argue it began in 140 CE, others in 142 CE. Recent archaeological work suggests the wall may have been intermittently occupied or abandoned and reoccupied, complicating the narrative of continuous Roman expansion. The Historia Augusta is now recognized as containing significant legendary material. The exact date of Vologases III's accession is disputed among scholars.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 142 CE is dominated by Roman and Chinese sources, reflecting the bias of literate, centralized states. The Parthian Empire is known primarily through Roman and Chinese accounts, which are often hostile or incomplete. The sources for this year are largely compiled centuries later, introducing the risk of anachronism and legendary embellishment. Archaeological evidence provides a crucial corrective, particularly for Britain and the Silk Road. The perspectives of non-literate peoples and regions are entirely absent from the surviving record.

graph_edges.note: The beginning of Antonine Wall construction in 142 CE leads to continued construction in 143 CE
Emperor Andi's reign in 142 CE leads to the accession of Emperor Zhidi in 143 CE
Parthian control of Silk Road enables Roman embassy to China in 166 CE
